summary: O'Neil calculates that approximately 578 New York City teachers per year will be arbitrarily denied tenure due to the statistical noise inherent in Value-Added Model (VAM) scores. Building on a previous thought experiment, she uses data showing 16% of teachers are rated 'ineffective' and estimates that 12.7% of tenure decisions will be wrongly negative due to VAM's unreliability. Starting with roughly 7,000 new teachers hired annually and accounting for 35% attrition before tenure review, she arrives at 4,550 candidates—of whom 578 will be unfairly punished by what she characterizes as a 'random number generator.'
key_points: About 16% of NYC teachers are rated 'ineffective' under the VAM system across multiple years
After four years, an estimated 12.7% of teachers will be arbitrarily denied tenure based solely on VAM score variability
With approximately 4,550 teachers going up for tenure annually, this translates to 578 people wrongly denied
The calculation doesn't account for broader damage to teacher morale and instruction quality from standardized testing pressures
money_quotes: About 578 people will be denied tenure simply based on this random number generator we call VAM.
This says nothing about the hard-to-measure damage done to all the good teachers trying to teach their kids but having to deal with this standardized testing nonsense.
It's a wonder anyone is willing to work here.
key_insight: Behind the seeming objectivity of teacher evaluation algorithms lies statistical noise that will arbitrarily derail hundreds of careers each year in New York City alone.
